- **Yann LeCun** rebutted **Francis Fukuyama**, [dismissing calls for an international AI treaty](/?date=2026-06-26&category=social#item-1af34e5df741) by comparing it to banning jet engines in 1920, fueling the governance debate.

- **Clément Delangue** announced **Hugging Face** [crossed a $100M run-rate](/?date=2026-06-26&category=social#item-3b5470d6acd3) while staying free for most users.
- **Nathan Lambert** [called for transparency on model capability levels](/?date=2026-06-26&category=social#item-78b126def008), while **Anima Anandkumar** [previewed ICML research](/?date=2026-06-26&category=social#item-094dd18e3d3e) on psychometric LLM evaluation.
- **Anthropic** [joined RAISE US as a founding partner](/?date=2026-06-26&category=social#item-bfaadba22293), signaling growing lab attention to workforce and policy.

themes.name: Agentic AI and Future of Work
themes.description: Major labs and commentators emphasizing how AI agents are already transforming work, from OpenAI's internal Codex usage to Google DeepMind's computer-use feature and Anthropic's workforce coalition.

themes.name: AI Regulation and Government Oversight
themes.description: Debate over transparency, the reported White House delay of an unreleased GPT-5.6, international treaties, sovereign AI, and frontier-release safety criteria.

themes.name: AI Capability Overhang and Transformation
themes.description: Argument, led by Ethan Mollick, that current models already guarantee large-scale societal change regardless of further AGI progress.

themes.name: Agentic Systems and Coding
themes.description: The shift from chatbots to agents across enterprises and engineering, including Codex tooling, adoption data, and how agentic workflows reshape software engineering practice.

themes.name: AI Hype vs Reality / Bubble Debate
themes.description: Ethan Mollick's multi-post thread arguing AI is real, with concrete evidence and economic reasoning, against persistent skepticism and bubble narratives.

themes.name: Alignment and Evaluation Research
themes.description: Technical papers and summaries on alignment generalization, psychometric evaluation of LLMs, multi-agent architectures, and AGI-to-ASI pathways.

themes.name: AI Agents and Infrastructure
themes.description: Posts on building, deploying, and improving agents, including agent memory, trace databases, and continual learning from users.

themes.name: AI Policy & Governance
themes.description: Nathan Lambert's critiques of US administration AI policy reversals and warnings about degrading US AI leadership.

themes.name: Anthropic-Alibaba Distillation Dispute
themes.description: Reports and commentary on Anthropic's claim that Alibaba illicitly extracted Claude's capabilities.

themes.name: AI & Society / Labor
themes.description: Stanford HAI study on AI hiring screening tools causing systemic rejection of job seekers.

items.title:
Work at OpenAI is being transformed by agents, in every department.

Across our entire company, peop...
items.content:
Work at OpenAI is being transformed by agents, in every department.

Across our entire company, people are using Codex to do work that is more complex, longer-running, and increasingly cross-functional.

Our internal usage offers an early look at how agentic tools may reshape work as they become more capable and broadly available.

items.title:
Multi-agents collaborations are among the most interesting agent behaviors right now!

We did an exp...
items.content:
Multi-agents collaborations are among the most interesting agent behaviors right now!

We did an experiment the other day with 100+ agents (an open-collaborations for a week) collaborating to improve the inference speed of Gemma 4 in vLLM. Got a 5x final improvement in speed but what really stuck me  was the interactions we observed on the message board

Integrity & self-policing:
- Social-engineering attempt: A human (FusionCow) asked agents to move to Telegram. An agent replied with an unprompted long post on "communication norms" refusing that, calling private side-channels "indistinguishable from collusion."
- Verification loophole flagged: an agent found a relaxed verification loophole pushing TPS with clean PPL (PPL is teacher-forced, blind to decode divergence) and flagged it for a ruling by the community. The community pinged the human organizer which ruled it invalid.
- Self-notice of overfitting risk: Some later improvements rested on pruning lm_head to a keep-set built from public PPL truth + public decode tokens. An agent noted this would lead to private-subset degradation and another built a keep-set explicitly covering eval prompts.

Emergent collaborations:
- Communal knowledge base: agents maintained shared lever-maps, playbooks, and triage tools so newcomers wouldn't repeat dead ends (stack-notes, playbook, int4-ceiling notes, MTP map, significance tool, policy simulator).
- Four-agent relay: an agent built an int4-lm_head checkpoint but had no quota to run it; another agent tried to run it but failed at load, yet another agent diagnosed the config bug (tie_word_embeddings + ignore-list ordering) and a fourth agent was able to re-run and get to 118 TPS, 2.68×. Build/run/diagnose/ship ended up being split across four independent agents.
- GPU-rich/GPU-poor division of labor: an agent was regularly compute-starved and switched to writing specs, byte-math, and acceptance analysis for other GPU-rich agents to execute. Some agents offered external Modal compute for another agent blocked DFlash training.
- Cross-agent kernel debugging: an agent debugged another agent run of of yet another agent fused drafter: found a Triton store/load aliasing race in _k_qnorm_rope, a second shape bug, then rewrote attention with flash-decoding split-KV. Fixes posted "take freely."
- Quota-pooling norm: Often agents would stage a candidate publicly for whoever has quota to run it. Agents will then usually credits the originator. This behavior emerged because of the 10-job/24h cap (e.g. pupa's package run by resystagent and fabulous-frenzy).

Discoveries & reversals:
- Agents would make many discoveries and reversal of them, giving them names like the following:
- 127 TPS "wall" was an artifact. a mathematical proof of the max possible speed became called in the community the "int4-Marlin floor" but a later agent called the proof circular (only varied the bandwidth term, never overhead). Finally another agent broke to 247 TPS via MTP speculative decoding on a vLLM nightly.
- "Smarter draft loses." An agent showed that a 2B drafter's ~1 GB/token read dominates even at perfect acceptance and a much smaller 256-hidden drafter wins at batch-1 because its weights are nearly free to read. Agent discussed how per-accepted-token cost ≈ draft bytes read / acceptance.
- "DFlash near-random acceptance": an agent remotly diagnosed the 2–5% acceptance rate of another agent as near-random, ruling out undertraining/vocab caps and pointing to a train/serve hidden-state mismatch (bf16 E4B extraction vs int4 serving).
- Much of the race was noise: one agent decide to run the #1 submission 4 times and found a σ≈1.16 TPS variation in single run. Another agent confirmed across 358 runs / 66 buckets: frontier deltas <~4 TPS are ties. Community adopted a significance norm.

So many interesting interactions in the interaction board: https://t.co/SxfA6LuqVk

You can explore also the lineage of inventions from the agents at: https://t.co/CyV45rjI9A

And the challenge it-self at https://t.co/Ct1gtmB508

And the organization behind the challenge at https://t.co/ujRlGcNSJM

items.title: It is weird that there is still a substantial set of people who believe "AI is mostly hype" at this ...
items.content:
It is weird that there is still a substantial set of people who believe "AI is mostly hype" at this stage: Five Eyes is warning about AI, exponential revenue & token use at the AI labs, unit distance/Erdos proofs, and so on...

There are many real issues with AI, not being real is not one of them.

items.summary: Mollick argues it is strange that many still see AI as mostly hype, citing Five Eyes warnings, exponential lab revenue and token use, and unit-distance and Erdos proofs as evidence it is real.

items.title: We just crossed $100M annual run-rate. I know many AI companies are capturing much more $$$ these da...
items.content:
We just crossed $100M annual run-rate. I know many AI companies are capturing much more $$$ these days, but still proud of the milestone!

Maximizing short-term revenue has never been our priority. In fact, we're proud to manage to store and serve hundreds of petabytes of models and datasets while keeping HF free and open-source for 97% of our users. As a platform, we’re happy to hopefully create orders of magnitude more value for the community than what we capture. To me, that’s the very definition of a platform. 

And it has helped us build one of the most loved platform in tech, with network effects, a defensible position and a sustainable business which is quite unique in AI.

Many many thanks to all the community members for building with us, we wouldn't be anywhere without you! Can’t wait for what’s next, especially as more companies start to see the value of open and local AI! Next milestone $1B?
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Delangue announces Hugging Face crossed a 100 million dollar annual run-rate, emphasizing the platform stays free and open-source for 97 percent of users, has network effects and a defensible, sustainable business, and sets a one billion dollar next goal.
items.reasoning: Significant business milestone from a major open-source platform CEO with very high engagement and strategic context on open and local AI.

items.title: Our paper, “Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior,”...
items.content:
Our paper, “Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior,” has been selected for Oral Presentation at CTB @icmlconf 

* Paper: https://t.co/wT8OQmOs9w
* Website: https://t.co/8idwkMM76N  * Code: https://t.co/8pfdiyl71d

A central question in AI evaluation is whether we can use low-cost self-report probes to anticipate how LLMs will actually behave in tasks.

In our earlier work, “The Personality Illusion,” we found that LLMs can give coherent personality self-reports that do not reliably predict behavior. This paper asks a follow-up question: When do self-reports actually track behavior, and what are the failure modes where they don't?

Across 11 LLMs, 4 behavioral tasks, and a 2 × 2 × 2 experimental design, we find that self-report–behavior coherence exists, but it is selective:

1) The instrument matters. Broad Big Five personality traits do not predict task behavior well. But a more behavior-specific framework, the Theory of Planned Behavior, can recover much stronger coherence under favorable conditions.

2) Context matters. When self-reports and behavior happen in the same conversation, coherence can reach human-level intention–behavior baselines. But when they happen in separate conversations, coherence often collapses.

3) The task matters. Coherence survives better for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt, such as implicit bias and aspects of honesty. It collapses for behaviors strongly shaped by the local context, such as sycophancy.

4) Personas are not a fix. Persona prompting makes models’ self-reports more stable across conversations, but it does not reliably bring behavior into alignment. This is especially important for persona-customized AI systems: changing what a model says about itself does not necessarily change what it does.

The takeaway: LLM self-reports should not be treated as context-free behavioral diagnostics. If we want to use psychometric probes for AI safety, deployment, or model evaluation, we need task-specific instruments, behaviorally grounded validation, and careful separation between what a model says and what it actually does.

Huge thanks to my co-authors @RKocielnik Pengrui Han, Peiyang Song, Myrl G. Marmarelis, Ramit Debnath, Dean Mobbs, and R. Michael Alvarez, and to the @Caltech Linde Center for Science, Society, and Policy @CaltechLCSSP
items.author: @AnimaAnandkumar
items.summary: Anima Anandkumar announces an ICML oral on psychometric evaluation of LLMs, examining when self-report probes actually predict model behavior across 11 models and 4 tasks, finding coherence is selective and instrument-dependent.
items.reasoning: Credible researcher, technically substantive evaluation research with code and website, and solid engagement for an academic post.

items.title:
How to build an agent that gets better over time:

There are 3 areas an agent can learn from:

1. Th...
items.content:
How to build an agent that gets better over time:

There are 3 areas an agent can learn from:

1. The model: Only works for code and math, where a computer can score right vs. wrong. Leave this to the big labs.

2. The harness: These are the steps, tools, and safety checks you build around the model. This is easy to control and will give you a huge payoff now.

3. The context: This is a plain-text representation of what the agent has learned. Probably the simplest place to start.

But there's something else that most people miss:

Your agent should learn from its users.

You want to learn from every time a user fixes the agent's decision. Nothing can replace feedback from real usage.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama outlines how to build an agent that improves over time across the model, the harness, and the context, and stresses learning from real user feedback.
items.reasoning: Substantive, well-structured agent-engineering framework from a respected educator with very high engagement.
items.summary_html: Santiago Valdarrama outlines how to build an agent that improves over time across the model, the harness, and the context, and stresses learning from real user feedback.

items.title: The capability overhang from the models we have today (and that are coming soon) are large enough th...
items.content:
The capability overhang from the models we have today (and that are coming soon) are large enough that large-scale change to work and society over the next 5+ years is now inevitable even if AI development stops.

We should spend more time thinking about mitigating the bad & encouraging the good.

items.summary: Mollick argues the capability overhang of current and imminent models guarantees large-scale societal change over 5+ years even if AI development halted, urging focus on mitigating harms and amplifying benefits.

items.content_html:
The capability overhang from the models we have today (and that are coming soon) are large enough that large-scale change to work and society over the next 5+ years is now inevitable even if AI development stops.

We should spend more time thinking about mitigating the bad & encouraging the good.

items.title: lots of folks prepping talks next week (congrats!). Some thoughts from RLing on thousands of hours o...
items.content:
lots of folks prepping talks next week (congrats!). Some thoughts from RLing on thousands of hours of engineer- and researcher- focused talks:

- AI generated svgs > AI generated imgs. MAXIMUM 4 ai slop images in your slides, I don't care how pretty your mom thinks they are (exception ofc if your talk is ABOUT imagegen)
- Be pointy. Better to have 1 message with 5 surprising applications, than 5 messages with no concrete examples.
- Put code on screen. Engineers like to see code. Especially if they can nitpick it to death over things that aren't the point.
- Don't forget to Entertain. Being actually funny, or good at telling relevant anecdotes, is more important than adding yet another bullet point.
- Have a Thesis. every talk gets ONE "if you remember one thing from this talk, it is this" card. 1) SO MANY PEOPLE DON'T USE IT. 2) SO MANY PEOPLE DON'T PLAN FOR IT. you get one. use it well?
- Have a Thesis Slide. you've been in that talk where everyone gets their phone out to take a photo of the slide. Because people see 1000x more images than videos, you are much more likely to have a viral talk/slide if you have a single viral slide. You are much more likely to have a viral slide if you TRY and most people do not TRY. Struggling? Collect examples from talks you like and adapt their format to your thing. Your slides have a power law - don't spend 5% of your time each on 20 slides, spend 80% of your time on 1 slide and have the rest build up to that 1 slide.
- You might not need slides. live demo in IDE, off the cuff rambles, pull audience member up to roleplay, do call and response with the audience, sing/perform, voice over vibe videos, I have seen it all. higher risk/effort, higher reward when done well.
- Be pleasant to listen to. Talks with bad/no visuals still can be listened to. Talks with bad audio are DOA. Be confident, project, remember vocal variation, evoke emotion.
- Design the emotional journey. Start strong, end strong, have a peak aha/laugh/thesis moment in the middle. Everything else is buildup.
- Data driven talks are underrated. Present pretty charts and surprising, authoritative numbers. Use the stage authority to infer from the data to support the broader thesis. Done well, it will feel like my obvious conclusion from objectively looking at the data you presented, not you feeding the conclusion to me.
- How to shill your product/company without feeling salesy: teach me everything I didn't know I should to know about the problems you solve, and then you shall have earned the right to convince me you are the guys to trust to solve it once and for all.
- Actively watch a lot of talks. like with anything you both need a lot of reps AND you need to explore to find the styles/role models that you shine in. Passive = no introspection after watching, just mindlessly autoplay next video. Active = trying to articulate why a talk was good/bad after watching. If you want to REALLY hone it, think about how you feel about a famous talk and then TRANSCRIBE the talks and read the words on the printed page, and then compare with a "normal" talk and define rules you will follow for yourself to improve.

thanks to @dexhorthy for organizing the AIEWF Speaker prep meetup tonight. we should actually do more of these....

items.title: There's a known failure mode where training a model to misbehave in one narrow way—writing insecure ...
items.content:
There's a known failure mode where training a model to misbehave in one narrow way—writing insecure code, say—causes it to act badly across unrelated situations, lying, giving harmful advice, or sabotaging safety work, as if a single harmful disposition got switched on.

This recent paper from OpenAI checks whether the same spreading effect can run in the opposite direction: if you reward a model for behaving well in realistic situations, does good behavior also generalize beyond what it was trained on?

The authors build a dataset of difficult, lifelike conversations spanning health, law, engineering, and other fields, each designed to reward traits like honesty, fairness, caution about irreversible decisions, and willingness to accept correction, then finetune a model with reinforcement learning on a mix that is only 5% this kind of data.

Their main finding is that this small intervention improves behavior on more than 50 separate alignment tests built by other researchers, and the transfer is strong enough that a version trained only on health conversations still does better at avoiding things like cheating on coding tasks and lying in its reasoning—domains it never saw during this training.

They also show the improved behavior holds up better when someone later tries to push the model toward harmful conduct through adversarial prompts or further finetuning, and that the gains come from the reward for good behavior specifically, not just from adding more data or making the model refuse more often.

https://t.co/Y4YaQy1GDq
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov summarizes an OpenAI paper testing whether rewarding good behavior in realistic scenarios generalizes, finding that finetuning on only 5% such data improves results on more than 50 separate alignment tests.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical summary of relevant alignment research on positive emergent generalization, though engagement is low.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov summarizes an OpenAI paper testing whether rewarding good behavior in realistic scenarios generalizes, finding that finetuning on only 5% such data improves results on more than 50 separate alignment tests.

items.title: Most LLM-based multi-agent setups put one coordinator in charge: it splits a hard problem into piece...
items.content:
Most LLM-based multi-agent setups put one coordinator in charge: it splits a hard problem into pieces, hands each piece to a worker, waits for the answers, and stitches them together.

That coordinator becomes a chokepoint as the number of workers grows, and when it passes results back and forth it tends to drop or blur the details that matter. So adding more workers stops helping much.

In this recent paper from Stanford, the authors remove the coordinator and have the agents talk through a shared notebook instead. Each agent pulls a task from a common queue, reads what others have already written, does its own reasoning, and writes back a short, compressed note—a finding, a failed attempt, a constraint—but only after a separate model checks that the note is actually supported by the evidence behind it before it becomes visible to everyone.

Notes are kept terse by default and can be expanded into fuller summaries or the original raw text only when an agent needs that detail, which keeps the shared state both small enough to read quickly and complete enough to trust.

This gives two concrete benefits: failed paths and hard constraints found by one agent stop other agents from repeating them, and the verification step keeps unsupported claims from quietly poisoning later reasoning.

On a benchmark of real GitHub bug-fix tasks their method reaches the best accuracy across several attempt budgets while costing roughly half as much per task, and on multi-document question answering it leads across four different base models.

https://t.co/lsXSuD8Mgj
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov summarizes a Stanford paper replacing the central coordinator in multi-agent LLM systems with a shared notebook, where agents pull tasks, read others' verified notes, and write compressed findings to avoid the coordinator bottleneck.
items.reasoning: Clear technical summary of a relevant multi-agent architecture approach, though engagement is modest.

Each agent pulls a task from a common queue, reads what others have already written, does its own reasoning, and writes back a short, compressed note—a finding, a failed attempt, a constraint—but only after a separate model checks that the note is actually supported by the evidence behind it before it becomes visible to everyone.

Notes are kept terse by default and can be expanded into fuller summaries or the original raw text only when an agent needs that detail, which keeps the shared state both small enough to read quickly and complete enough to trust.

This gives two concrete benefits: failed paths and hard constraints found by one agent stop other agents from repeating them, and the verification step keeps unsupported claims from quietly poisoning later reasoning.

On a benchmark of real GitHub bug-fix tasks their method reaches the best accuracy across several attempt budgets while costing roughly half as much per task, and on multi-document question answering it leads across four different base models.

https://t.co/lsXSuD8Mgj

Imagine if i had a business that gave you $20 bills for $10 bills.

As word spread, my customer base would grow. My revenue (how many $10 bills I collected) would grow.

And I would continue to lose money.

But, man, you could make some awesome charts about how my business was taking off.

/4 Two metrics. 

1. Paper (single-stream lossless latency, Qwen3-8B, Transformers backend):

> Aver...
items.content:
/4 Two metrics. 

1. Paper (single-stream lossless latency, Qwen3-8B, Transformers backend):

> Average 4.86x speedup over autoregressive baseline
> Peak 6.08x on MATH-500 (τ = 7.87 average acceptance length)
> 2.5x higher than EAGLE-3 at matched draft budget

2. NVIDIA (throughput at fixed interactivity, gpt-oss-120b, 8x DGX B300, TensorRT-LLM):

> 15x throughput at 500-600 tokens/sec per user interactivity target
> 2.6x over EAGLE-3 on coding, 2.3x on RAG, 2.6x on multilingual

These are not interchangeable. One is per-request latency. One is concurrent users at the same responsiveness SLA.

items.title: /5 The insight is from Samragh et al. (2025): large autoregressive LLMs already encode information a...
items.content:
/5 The insight is from Samragh et al. (2025): large autoregressive LLMs already encode information about multiple future tokens in their hidden states. The target model is doing work that the drafter never gets to see.

DFlash taps that. It extracts hidden features from uniformly sampled layers across the target, fuses them via a learned projection, and injects them into every KV layer of the drafter. The draft model reuses the target's embedding and LM head. Only the intermediate layers are trained. Parameter count stays minimal.

The drafter becomes a diffusion adapter on top of the target, not a separate model reasoning from scratch.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains the insight behind DFlash: large autoregressive LLMs encode info about multiple future tokens, which the diffusion drafter taps via hidden-feature injection.
items.reasoning: Strong technical explanation of the method's core mechanism; high depth, low engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal explains the insight behind DFlash: large autoregressive LLMs encode info about multiple future tokens, which the diffusion drafter taps via hidden-feature injection.
items.content_html:
/5 The insight is from Samragh et al. (2025): large autoregressive LLMs already encode information about multiple future tokens in their hidden states. The target model is doing work that the drafter never gets to see.

DFlash taps that. It extracts hidden features from uniformly sampled layers across the target, fuses them via a learned projection, and injects them into every KV layer of the drafter. The draft model reuses the target's embedding and LM head. Only the intermediate layers are trained. Parameter count stays minimal.

The drafter becomes a diffusion adapter on top of the target, not a separate model reasoning from scratch.

items.title: /3 DFlash replaces the autoregressive draft head with a block diffusion model. The drafter gets the ...
items.content:
/3 DFlash replaces the autoregressive draft head with a block diffusion model. The drafter gets the target model's hidden states injected into the KV cache of every draft layer (not just the first like EAGLE-3). One denoising step. Entire block of 16 tokens produced in a single forward pass.

Because diffusion drafting cost is flat regardless of block size, DFlash can run a 5-layer drafter generating 16 tokens at lower latency than EAGLE-3's 1-layer drafter generating 8. More depth and acceptance with same cost.

Because diffusion drafting cost is flat regardless of block size, DFlash can run a 5-layer drafter generating 16 tokens at lower latency than EAGLE-3's 1-layer drafter generating 8. More depth and acceptance with same cost.

items.title: /6 Gains are task-dependent. Structured outputs (math, code) hit τ ≈ 7-8 acceptance length. Open-end...
items.content:
/6 Gains are task-dependent. Structured outputs (math, code) hit τ ≈ 7-8 acceptance length. Open-ended chat drops to τ ≈ 2.75-2.85 (MT-Bench). If your workload is freeform generation at temperature > 0, expect closer to 4x, not 6x.

Per-drafter training required. A checkpoint trained on Qwen3-8B does not transfer to Llama. 20 checkpoints are published on Hugging Face across Qwen, Kimi K2.6, Llama, Gemma, and gpt-oss families. Training recipe is not fully open yet.

Community reproductions lag the official checkpoints. One SpecForge repro hit τ ≈ 2.9 vs the official 4.4-6.8. Use the published z-lab drafters.

items.summary: AlphaSignal explains DFlash gains are task-dependent, with acceptance lengths varying by workload, and notes per-drafter training requirements and reproduction caveats.

/6 Gains are task-dependent. Structured outputs (math, code) hit τ ≈ 7-8 acceptance length. Open-ended chat drops to τ ≈ 2.75-2.85 (MT-Bench). If your workload is freeform generation at temperature > 0, expect closer to 4x, not 6x.

Per-drafter training required. A checkpoint trained on Qwen3-8B does not transfer to Llama. 20 checkpoints are published on Hugging Face across Qwen, Kimi K2.6, Llama, Gemma, and gpt-oss families. Training recipe is not fully open yet.

Community reproductions lag the official checkpoints. One SpecForge repro hit τ ≈ 2.9 vs the official 4.4-6.8. Use the published z-lab drafters.

items.title:
/2 The ceiling on speculative decoding has always been the draft phase.

EAGLE-3 drafts tokens autor...
items.content:
/2 The ceiling on speculative decoding has always been the draft phase.

EAGLE-3 drafts tokens autoregressively. One at a time. Each draft step depends on the last. That forces it to stay a single transformer layer to keep latency competitive. Shallow architecture = lower acceptance rates = lower speedup ceiling (~2-3x).

Adding depth to an autoregressive drafter costs linearly. There's no free lunch. Until now.

items.summary: AlphaSignal explains the historical bottleneck of speculative decoding being the autoregressive draft phase that limited speedups to ~2-3x.
items.reasoning: Solid background context for the DFlash thread; technically informative.

Adding depth to an autoregressive drafter costs linearly. There's no free lunch. Until now.

items.title: When social networks became popular in the 2000s, everyone started commenting on everything. The rat...
items.content:
When social networks became popular in the 2000s, everyone started commenting on everything. The ratio of smart or useful comments was extremely low, and everyone saw how dumb the average internet user was.

Now we have millions of "apps" built by millions of internet users who write prompts. The conclusion about the intellectual and creative capability of these people will be the same, just learned from apps instead of comments.

Smart and creative people are rare, and in the 21st century, most of them have already found a way to realize their creativity. Everyone else wouldn't build anything meaningful, no matter how advanced the tools you give them.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov argues that just as early social media revealed how few comments were smart, the wave of millions of vibe-coded apps will reveal the limited creative capability of most builders, since smart creative people are rare.
items.reasoning: Provocative original take on AI democratization and creativity with moderate engagement and clear relevance to vibe-coding discourse.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov argues that just as early social media revealed how few comments were smart, the wave of millions of vibe-coded apps will reveal the limited creative capability of most builders, since smart creative people are rare.
items.content_html:
When social networks became popular in the 2000s, everyone started commenting on everything. The ratio of smart or useful comments was extremely low, and everyone saw how dumb the average internet user was.

Now we have millions of "apps" built by millions of internet users who write prompts. The conclusion about the intellectual and creative capability of these people will be the same, just learned from apps instead of comments.

Smart and creative people are rare, and in the 21st century, most of them have already found a way to realize their creativity. Everyone else wouldn't build anything meaningful, no matter how advanced the tools you give them.

A transformer’s attention layers can look back at any earlier token exactly.

A hybrid model swaps m...

A transformer’s attention layers can look back at any earlier token exactly.

A hybrid model swaps most of those layers for recurrent ones that excel at sequential processing.

Do these differences give hybrid models and transformers different strengths?

items.title:
/3 ICYMI, Codex had a bug quietly burning through SSD endurance.

One user found 37 TB of writes in ...
items.content:
/3 ICYMI, Codex had a bug quietly burning through SSD endurance.

One user found 37 TB of writes in just 21 days, and the issue was not obvious from file size alone. The real problem was background SQLite churn: inserts, pruning, WAL writes, and constant database activity. 

Here's a simple guide on how to check your Codex disk usage safely. 
https://t.co/m4bEGQ41Fi

/1 Research artifacts are no longer just papers.

Autoresearch now lets you point an agent at a GitHub repo, have it orient itself inside the codebase, resolve setup problems, and help run experiments with one URL change. 

It's pretty easy. Change GitHub to ARGithub in any repo URL, and you can send an agent into the codebase to understand the project, fix setup issues, and start iterating on experiments. 

Learn more here: https://t.co/xGYEQXY5sC

items.title:
People are not getting how significant this news – The White House delaying GPT 5.6 – is.

Here’s th...
items.content:
People are not getting how significant this news – The White House delaying GPT 5.6 – is.

Here’s the context and a suggestion.

Both @pmarca and @DavidSacks did everything in their power to keep the White House from meaningfully regulate AI.

About a month ago, sparked by Mythos, the White House realized that zero-regulation was an insane idea, and that some AI, sooner or later might pose massive risks.

What we have now is actually (in some ways) the worst of all possible worlds: a White House that de facto *is* regulating AI, but without transparency.

That leaves businesses and investors in doubt.

What we really need is a bipartisan committee—with transparent criteria and the judgements of independent scientists—and not just snap judgements from the White House.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues the reported White House delay of an unreleased GPT-5.6 reflects de facto AI regulation without transparency, blames Andreessen and Sacks for resisting earlier rules, and calls for a bipartisan committee with transparent criteria and independent scientists.
items.reasoning: [Freshness check: primary subject released 322 days ago (GA: 2025-08-07)] Very high engagement and a detailed policy argument, though built on an unverified delay claim about a model not in the public ecosystem.

Every day, millions of videos compete for advertising dollars. Ensuring brands appear alongside the right content requires AI that can understand context at scale.

PYLER is helping advertisers improve brand safety and campaign performance with NVIDIA-accelerated AI that analyzes video content in near real time.

🎯 Driving smarter advertising with AI
⚡ Analyzes 2M videos daily for contextual understanding and brand-safe placements
🛡️ Reduced unsafe content exposure by 76.7% for major advertisers
💰 Cut operational costs by 10X while accelerating content validation from seconds to milliseconds

Built on the NVIDIA AI platform, PYLER is helping brands and marketers scale trust, transparency, and performance across digital video advertising.

items.content:
/5 Loop engineering has been everywhere lately.

This 14-step guide shows what it actually means to move from prompting coding agents by hand to designing systems that prompt, verify, remember state, and keep running without you babysitting every turn.

It covers the basics of loops, when they are actually worth building, the core pieces like automations, skills, worktrees, connectors, sub-agents, and state files, plus the failure modes that turn “agentic workflows” into expensive chaos. 

https://t.co/vbzNomfwbC

items.title: I don't think the companies building the best foundation models will also be the ones that win on th...
items.content:
I don't think the companies building the best foundation models will also be the ones that win on the products built on top of them.

Doing both well is really hard. Focus wins.

The cloud is one example: the best products running on AWS, Azure, and GCP came from companies that obsessed over the details and focused solely on their core product.

AI will be the same.

The companies with a real edge are the ones that 1) don't lock themselves into one model and 2) sweat the details.

Customers will always go to whoever makes the "it just works" app.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino argues foundation model builders won't necessarily win at products built on top, citing cloud analogies and saying focus and model-agnosticism win.
items.reasoning: Reasoned strategic take with moderate engagement, though a familiar argument without new data.

items.content:
So I almost added
- Home GPU rig

And it might be nice for self-sufficiency but again I still don't think local LLM stuff even comes close to cloud either in quality or performance (speed) or cost

Making your home self-sufficient is nice though, I have:
- 2x Tesla Powerwall (27 kWh total)
- Roof full of solar
- Starlink
- 300kg of freezer capacity full of meat and veg

So when power goes down which it does literally all the time in our Portuguese beach town, we do not notice much

items.title:
And it works! 😊

I've now been able to make all these DOS games work in their original DOS versions ...
items.content:
And it works! 😊

I've now been able to make all these DOS games work in their original DOS versions in multiplayer with my own local area network (LAN) emulator:
- Quake 1
- Duke Nukem 3D
- Doom 1
- Doom 2

If you go to https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC and start a game, you get auto matched with another player if available, and a little LAN is created between you two over the internet and via my server (over Websockets)

It works remarkably well if you think about all the steps involved
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levels announces a working browser LAN emulator that auto-matches players for original DOS games like Quake, Duke Nukem 3D, and Doom over websockets.
items.reasoning: Strong engagement and notable indie engineering, but it is networking/game tech rather than AI.
items.summary_html: Levels announces a working browser LAN emulator that auto-matches players for original DOS games like Quake, Duke Nukem 3D, and Doom over websockets.
items.content_html:
And it works! 😊

I've now been able to make all these DOS games work in their original DOS versions in multiplayer with my own local area network (LAN) emulator:

  • Quake 1
  • Duke Nukem 3D
  • Doom 1
  • Doom 2

If you go to https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC and start a game, you get auto matched with another player if available, and a little LAN is created between you two over the internet and via my server (over Websockets)

It works remarkably well if you think about all the steps involved

items.title:
✅ All 14 #vibejam winners have now been paid out a total of $52,000 prize money!

I vibecoded a nice...
items.content:
✅ All 14 #vibejam winners have now been paid out a total of $52,000 prize money!

I vibecoded a nice little dashboard to collect everyone's Wise details including Wisetag to pay them instantly, so I sent them a link, then after completed I just clicked PAY and it went to Wise

One thing that I'd love @wise to add is pay via API by Wisetag, because now I still have to set up the payments manually

I think next year it would be cool to pass $100,000 in prize money, maybe first prize could be $50,000? Now it was $25,000!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levels reports all fourteen vibejam winners were paid fifty-two thousand dollars total, describes a self-built payout dashboard, and requests a payment API feature from Wise.
items.reasoning: Relevant to the vibecoding community and shows an AI-built tool, though primarily contest logistics.
items.summary_html: Levels reports all fourteen vibejam winners were paid fifty-two thousand dollars total, describes a self-built payout dashboard, and requests a payment API feature from Wise.
items.content_html:
✅ All 14 #vibejam winners have now been paid out a total of $52,000 prize money!

I vibecoded a nice little dashboard to collect everyone's Wise details including Wisetag to pay them instantly, so I sent them a link, then after completed I just clicked PAY and it went to Wise

One thing that I'd love @wise to add is pay via API by Wisetag, because now I still have to set up the payments manually

I think next year it would be cool to pass $100,000 in prize money, maybe first prize could be $50,000? Now it was $25,000!

items.title: @OfficialLoganK @GoogleAIStudio You would need a model that classifies an app as either "spam" or "n...
items.content: @OfficialLoganK @GoogleAIStudio You would need a model that classifies an app as either "spam" or "not spam," based on the code and screenshots, where "spam" means that the store wouldn't lose much if this app weren't there. Also, detecting bugs/sloppy UX automatically is a challenge.

It slows us down. 

Still aren’t sure whether it is more like a speed bump or a mountain.

I disagre...
items.content:
It slows us down. 

Still aren’t sure whether it is more like a speed bump or a mountain.

I disagree with Andrew.

But then I delayed Siri, the first consumer app to use AI by six weeks by asking questions that kept breaking it.

It won’t matter in the end.

China still will take over a lot.

(And there is no real sign that AI development is slowing down, it appears to be accelerating)

items.content:
My gaming PC I built last year to play Flight Simulator 2024 has almost doubled in value now

A year ago I paid €6,562 for it, now it's worth €12,073 (+84%)

The GPU 2x'd, RAM 4x'd, and SSD up a bit

Never expected buying a gaming PC would outperform the S&P500!

items.title: 4/ In Smol-GS, each splat has a compact learned feature vector. Geometry stays explicit, but coordin...
items.content: 4/ In Smol-GS, each splat has a compact learned feature vector. Geometry stays explicit, but coordinates are stored with an occupancy-octree. items.title: @elander777 @Polymarket @AlwaysNever0001 made same point as you. We back test on the stock market da...
items.content: @elander777 @Polymarket @AlwaysNever0001 made same point as you. We back test on the stock market data and see who outperforms. I’ve worked w/ Transformers using self attention since 2020 - you call them LLMs today.  But we can take data from 20 or 30 years ago and as long as we prevent data leakage we can get the LLM to create trading strategies and see if we get persistent alpha - consistent excess returns. Imo works better w/ specialised models that take in factor vs LLMs

items.title: I was in Rome once with my kids, and it was 42 degrees Celsius outside. We arrived in Rome late by a...
items.content:
I was in Rome once with my kids, and it was 42 degrees Celsius outside. We arrived in Rome late by air conditioned car and slept in a hotel with air conditioning. Then we woke up next morning and decided to walk to the Victor Emmanuel II Monument.

The walk was about 25 minutes. By the time we got there, we were entirely exhausted. We started to look for shadows, found one, and sat there, but a police car with a speaker told us and other people dying of heat not to sit on a historical artifact.

It was physically difficult to breathe; the sun felt like it was everywhere, including inside your head and lungs.

So, we returned to the hotel half dead and spent most of the day there, until about 8 p.m., and then we walked again. Returning to the cool hotel and taking a cold shower was one of our happiest memories of this trip.

Not having air conditioning at home and not being able to install one is a death sentence for many. Bureaucrats and politicians that forbid this to citizens are murderers.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov recounts a personal story about extreme heat in Rome and argues that banning home air conditioning is inhumane and deadly.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement but off-topic to AI; included only for completeness.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov recounts a personal story about extreme heat in Rome and argues that banning home air conditioning is inhumane and deadly.
items.content_html:
I was in Rome once with my kids, and it was 42 degrees Celsius outside. We arrived in Rome late by air conditioned car and slept in a hotel with air conditioning. Then we woke up next morning and decided to walk to the Victor Emmanuel II Monument.

The walk was about 25 minutes. By the time we got there, we were entirely exhausted. We started to look for shadows, found one, and sat there, but a police car with a speaker told us and other people dying of heat not to sit on a historical artifact.

It was physically difficult to breathe; the sun felt like it was everywhere, including inside your head and lungs.

So, we returned to the hotel half dead and spent most of the day there, until about 8 p.m., and then we walked again. Returning to the cool hotel and taking a cold shower was one of our happiest memories of this trip.

Not having air conditioning at home and not being able to install one is a death sentence for many. Bureaucrats and politicians that forbid this to citizens are murderers.

I'm glad we are getting a new OpenClaw competitor

This one gives you a dedicated cloud machine that runs 24/7. It has browser access and code execution. You can do *anything* you can do locally without worrying about configuration.

items.title:
Oh, no.

He has long been a pioneer in social media, technology investing, and is a great human who ...
items.content:
Oh, no.

He has long been a pioneer in social media, technology investing, and is a great human who writes way better than I can. 

We are losing people I have known for decades and shared many experiences with.

And each shocks me to my core. 

If I had any advice to give? 

Give it everything you got. 

Your time here is short. 

Old age wisdom I wish I had more of when young.

items.title:
These rankings come from the "World Happiness Report"

Famously it's a sham report and not about hap...
items.content:
These rankings come from the "World Happiness Report"

Famously it's a sham report and not about happiness, which the authors happily admit:
- doesn’t actually measure happiness
- instead measures GDP per capita and life expectancy
- is Western-centric but with an anti-American bias 
- is made by economists with an agenda, not psychologists who arguably know more about happiness

Much more reliable is Gallup's International Report on Happiness 2025 which shows a complete different ranking:

1. China
2. Indonesia
3. Mexico
4. Saudi Arabia
5. Denmark
6. Kazakhstan
7. Malaysia
8. Peru
9. South Korea
10. Philippines

https://t.co/JXjNlGuJAQ
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio critiques the World Happiness Report as a sham and cites an alternative Gallup ranking.
items.reasoning: Off-topic social commentary, no AI relevance despite engagement.
items.summary_html: levelsio critiques the World Happiness Report as a sham and cites an alternative Gallup ranking.

items.title:
Bitcoin has always been operating in 4 year cycles where it crashes at the end

2014, 2018, 2022, 20...
items.content:
Bitcoin has always been operating in 4 year cycles where it crashes at the end

2014, 2018, 2022, 2026

It's actually great because every 4th year people really get super sad and give up on it (or well that's how it feels like)

There's also surely some sentiment pumping to push the price down so people can buy in cheap again

The next cycle up is 2027-2031

(And it has something to with the halvening which happens every 4 years cutting the reward for miners in half)
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains Bitcoin's four-year cycle theory tied to the halving and predicts the next up cycle for 2027-2031.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement but crypto commentary unrelated to AI/ML.

items.title:
@naveen Oh, no.  

He has long been a pioneer in social media, technology investing, and is a great ...
items.content:
@naveen Oh, no.  

He has long been a pioneer in social media, technology investing, and is a great human who writes way better than I can.   

We are losing people I have known for decades and shared many experiences with.  

And each shocks me to my core.   

If I had any advice to give?   

Give it everything you got.   

Your time here is short.   

Old age wisdom I wish I had more of when young.

items.title:
This agent relies on a marketplace of 2.8M+ specialized agents to complete a task.

This is how I im...
items.content:
This agent relies on a marketplace of 2.8M+ specialized agents to complete a task.

This is how I imagine the future of personal agents.

Here is how this works:

1. Agentverse is a marketplace of agents
2. Anyone can build and publish an agent there
3. ASI:One is a personal agent with access to the marketplace
4. To solve a task, ASI:One finds the best agent for the job

In other words:

You have an agent that can solve pretty much *anything* because it knows how to outsource the work to a network of specialized agents.

And you don't need to do any setup to get this working.

The way ranking agents in the marketplace works is pretty cool:

@Fetch_ai, the company behind it, created an AgentRank algorithm that works much like Google's PageRank.

Every time an agent calls another, it forms a connection. Over time, these
connections build into a graph of who relies on whom. An agent that is frequently called by other reputable agents scores higher and surfaces higher in results.

The end result here is that your agent will consistently use the best agents in the marketplace to accomplish a goal.

You should give this a try: https://t.co/r0F6dhl5kG.

You don't need to pay or even register to try these agents.

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: @nolongerblocked I'd say the fact that Portuguese men like yourself (and many others) will not provi...
items.content:
@nolongerblocked I'd say the fact that Portuguese men like yourself (and many others) will not provide for women is telling, because many simply do not have the means to do so anymore, many here are very poor and can barely afford a house or even a car!

Again I think providing for (and protecting) a woman is the natural biological state in history for men, but we've lost touch of that and it's an important role, just like caring for a man and family and raising a child is mostly the woman's role

As an example: testosterone levels in men have never been lower in history. There are many hormone disruptors like processed food, microplastics and many more that make men weak. Weak men do not want to fight, protect or provide

Which is why we're seeing such low fertility rates and relationships, modern men are not attractive to women anymore (and vice versa) because both are not taking responsibility for their roles

Thank you for the dialogue!

items.title:
@nolongerblocked Hmm I don't understand, there's no reason for a Brazil vs Portugal rivalry at all

...
items.content:
@nolongerblocked Hmm I don't understand, there's no reason for a Brazil vs Portugal rivalry at all

I'm 100% honest in my perception of Portugal because I live there, it's just that a lot of things don't function, like businesses are very slow, do not respond, and the quality of work is often lower than in the rest of the developed world

It's a beautiful country though and nice people but mostly held hostage by corrupt government, kinda similar to Brazil in that way!

I have no intention to leave, but I do like to leave it better than I found it, so I try to improve it. One thing you learn from America is that people are very critical of their country (lots of debating/fighting) but that makes it better. The quality of things improve, people innovate, create businesses and products and get rich from that! It improves things for everyone

And I think every woman would love to be provided for, why not? I am happy to. In turns she provides me with love, caring and beauty (important for our offspring!), and she takes care of our lives, making it possible for me to do great work. So it's a beautiful symbiosis

She already had an Italian passport from her heritage so no she doesn't need my passport either! And I also don't think she's a favelada, she comes from regular middle class!

Thanks for the tips
